title: How proprioception changes after prolonged dissociation
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
I couldn't feel my own skin

For years, I didn't understand why I kept walking into door frames. Why I'd bruise my hips on countertops and not notice until hours later. Why my handwriting got sloppy, why I dropped things, why I felt like I was piloting a body that didn't quite belong to me.

Here's the thing. I thought I was just clumsy. Spacey. Maybe a little depressed. But what I was actually experiencing was a breakdown in proprioception. That's the sense that tells you where your body is in space without you having to look. It's how you know your arm is raised even with your eyes closed. How you walk up stairs without staring at your feet. How you catch yourself before you fall.

And when you've been dissociated for a long time, this sense can get scrambled. Badly.

Proprioception relies on constant feedback from muscles, joints, and tendons to the brain. It's a two-way conversation. Your body sends signals. Your brain interprets them and sends movement instructions back. But dissociation interrupts that conversation. It's like someone put a hand over the receiver. The signals are still there. You just can't hear them clearly anymore.

Stephen Porges, the researcher behind Polyvagal Theory, would say this is a survival adaptation. When your nervous system perceives threat, it shifts into protective states. Dorsal vagal shutdown. Freeze. Collapse. And in those states, your brain deprioritizes body awareness. Why would it waste energy tracking your exact limb position when it thinks you're about to die? It won't. It can't. That energy goes to survival.

So your proprioception goes quiet. And you start living in a body that feels vague. Blurry. Like you're wearing someone else's skin.

Stay with me here. This isn't just about being clumsy. It's about how you experience being alive.

The body map gets erased

Peter Levine, who developed Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma fragments our body sense. He describes it as a loss of connection to the felt sense. That's the internal awareness of your body's position, tension, temperature, and movement. When dissociation is chronic, that felt sense can become almost nonexistent.

I remember lying in bed at night and feeling like my legs weren't attached to my torso. Like they were just these distant objects. I'd have to wiggle my toes to confirm they were still there. That's not normal. But for someone with a history of prolonged dissociation, it's terrifyingly common.

What's happening neurologically is complex. Your brain's insula, the region responsible for interoception (sensing internal body states), becomes less active during dissociation. The somatosensory cortex, which processes touch and position, also downregulates. Your brain literally stops paying attention to your body's signals because those signals were associated with pain. With threat. With overwhelm.

So your body map gets erased. Not permanently. But it gets covered in fog.

And this has real consequences. You might have trouble coordinating movement. You might feel dizzy or off-balance. You might struggle with tasks that require fine motor control, like writing or buttoning a shirt. You might bump into things constantly. You might have a poor sense of how much force to use, so you slam doors or break pencils or hug people too hard or not hard enough.

You might feel like a ghost in a machine.

I've been there. It's disorienting. It's lonely. And it makes you question whether you're even real sometimes.

But here's what I want you to know. Proprioception can be restored. It takes time and patience and a lot of weird exercises. But the body wants to come back online. It really does.

How to rebuild proprioception from the ground up

The first step is accepting that you might need to start at a very basic level. I'm talking kindergarten-level basic. And that's okay. You're not regressing. You're rebuilding a foundation that was never properly laid, or that got cracked under the weight of trauma.

I started with simple weight-shifting exercises. Standing with my feet hip-width apart and slowly moving my weight from one foot to the other. Feeling the pressure change in my soles. Noticing which parts of my feet were bearing weight. It felt ridiculous at first. But after a few days, I started to feel my feet more clearly. Like they were becoming more solid. More present.

Then I moved to balance work. Standing on one foot while holding a wall. Closing my eyes and seeing how long I could stay upright. Using a Breathing Exercise Tool to slow my exhale while I balanced, which helped calm my nervous system enough to actually feel my body instead of floating away from it.

Progressive muscle relaxation was another game changer. Tensing and releasing each muscle group from my toes to my scalp. Paying attention to the contrast between tension and relaxation. This is a classic technique, but when you're dissociated, it's not just relaxing. It's reestablishing communication with parts of your body that went silent.

I also started doing joint rotations. Circling my ankles, wrists, shoulders. Slowly. With my eyes closed. Noticing the range of motion. Feeling the click and glide of joints. This sounds so simple, but for someone whose brain has been ignoring proprioceptive input for years, it's like sending a signal that says "I'm here. I'm paying attention. Please start talking to me again."

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk. He explains exactly how trauma reshapes the brain and body, and why body-based approaches are so essential for recovery. It's not a light read. But it's validating in a way that made me feel less crazy.

Another one that helped me understand the connection between chronic stress and physical symptoms is When the Body Says No by Gabor Mate. He traces how suppressed emotions and chronic dissociation lead to real physical consequences. It's sobering. But it also made me realize that my body wasn't broken. It was protecting me. And now it needed me to help it learn that protection was no longer necessary.

I also started using Loop Quiet 2 Ear Plugs during my proprioception work. A discreet option for reducing noise sensitivity is to wear these while doing body-focused exercises. They dampen auditory input so I can turn my attention inward more easily. Less sensory overload means more capacity to feel my body.

The role of the nervous system in this process

Here's the thing about rebuilding proprioception after dissociation. It's not just a mechanical problem. It's a nervous system problem. You can't force your body to feel if your nervous system is still stuck in threat response.

Deb Dana, who writes extensively about Polyvagal Theory in practice, emphasizes that we need to work with the nervous system, not against it. If you try to do proprioceptive exercises while your system is still in freeze or fight-or-flight, you're going to hit a wall. Your brain won't allow body awareness because it perceives that awareness as dangerous.

So you have to start with regulation. Co-regulation with a safe person, if possible. Or self-regulation through breath, movement, and environment. You need to signal safety to your nervous system before it will let you feel your body.

That's why I emphasize the breathing tool. It's not a gimmick. Slowing your exhale activates the vagus nerve and shifts you toward the ventral vagal state. That's the state where social engagement and body awareness are possible. Without that shift, you're just going through the motions.

Janina Fisher, a trauma specialist and author of "Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors", talks about how different parts of us hold different body memories. Some parts might feel numb. Others might feel hyperaware. The goal isn't to get rid of any part. It's to help all parts feel safe enough to integrate.

So when I do proprioception work now, I check in with my nervous system first. Am I grounded? Is my breathing slow and even? Do I feel a sense of safety in my environment? If not, I stop. I do some orienting. I look around the room. I notice what's in my peripheral vision. I feel my feet on the floor. I wait until my system settles before I try to feel my body more deeply.

Wild, right? The paradox is that you have to feel safe before you can feel your body, but feeling your body is what helps you feel safe. It's a feedback loop. And it takes time to build.

What recovery feels like

I won't tell you that proprioception returns to normal quickly. It doesn't. For me, it's been a gradual process over years. Some days I feel solid. Present. Like I actually inhabit my body. Other days I still feel floaty and disconnected. Those days are harder. But they're less frequent now.

What changed was my relationship with my body. I stopped seeing it as a source of pain and confusion. I started seeing it as a partner. A collaborator. Something that had been trying to protect me in the only way it knew how.

I also stopped expecting perfection. My proprioception will never be as sharp as someone who never dissociated. And that's okay. I don't need it to be perfect. I just need it to be good enough to keep me connected to the world. To keep me from walking into door frames. To let me feel my own skin as mine.

If you're reading this and you recognize yourself, I see you. The clumsiness. The numbness. The feeling of being a stranger in your own body. It's real. It's not your fault. And it can get better.

Start small. Stand on one foot. Breathe slowly. Wiggle your toes. Notice what you notice. Don't force anything. Just invite your body back into your awareness. It might be hesitant at first. It might be scared. But it wants to come home. I promise you that.

Sit with that.

  • The Body Keeps the Score - A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful for understanding how trauma reshapes the body.
  • When the Body Says No - A book that connects the dots between stress and illness, and the role of dissociation in physical health.
